findings.description: Off-by-one: condition len(hits) <= self.limit permits one extra request, allowing limit+1 hits per window instead of limit.
Due to the off-by-one, after appending len(hits) can equal limit+1, making the reported remaining value negative (-1).
Trusting the client-supplied X-Forwarded-For first hop lets attackers spoof arbitrary client keys to bypass or evade the per-client rate limit.
No locking around prune/append on shared _hits state; concurrent gateway requests race, corrupting buckets and permitting over-limit bursts.
hits.pop(0) on a list is O(n); pruning many expired timestamps is O(n^2) per check — a collections.deque with popleft() would be O(1).
current_usage indexes self._hits[key] via defaultdict, creating an empty bucket as a side effect for unknown keys — read-only queries leak memory.
Buckets are only reclaimed by manual sweep(); with spoofable per-request keys and no automatic eviction, _hits grows unbounded (memory-exhaustion DoS).
Falling back to a literal 'unknown' key collapses all header-less clients into one shared bucket, causing cross-client throttling.
retry_after = hits[0] + window - now is not clamped to >= 0 and can return a small negative value near the window boundary.
Decision is constructed with positional arguments; keyword arguments would improve readability and guard against field reordering.
